edge: Neural networks are known to suffer from catastrophic forgetting, where the model’s performances on old tasks drop dramatically after being optimized for a new task.

The continual learning community has proposed several solutions aiming to equip the neural network with the ability to learn the current task (plasticity) while still achieving high accuracy on the previous tasks (stability).

In this work, we propose Auxiliary Network Continual Learning (ANCL), a novel method that applies an additional auxiliary network which promotes plasticity to the continually learned model which mainly focuses on stability.
The proposed framework materializes in a regularizer that naturally interpolates between plasticity and stability, surpassing strong baselines on task incremental and class incremental scenarios.
Through extensive analyses on ANCL solutions, we identify some essential principles beneath the stability-plasticity trade-off.
The continual learning (CL) model aims to learn from current data while still maintaining the information from previous training data.
current data is being compared with training data for maintaining information
naive approach leads to catastrophic forgetting due to continuous fine-tuning
catastrophic forgetting happens in gradient-based neural networks due to updates from current tasks overriding model weights from old tasks
catastrophic forgetting is related to the stability-plasticity dilemma in terms of continual learning challenges
model needs to balance stability (generalizing well on past data) and plasticity (learning new concepts) to avoid catastrophic forgetting
class incremental learning, task incremental learning, and domain incremental learning are categories of continual learning
class incremental learning and domain incremental learning are different scenarios in continual learning
task incremental learning involves being informed about the task identity while domain incremental learning doesn't require task identity
The model is required to solve only one task at hands without the task identity.
In CIL, the model should solve the task itself and infer the task identity.
The model in CIL should discriminate all classes that have been seen so far.
Our study performs extensive evaluations on TIL and CIL setting.
Several papers proposed the usage of an auxiliary network or an extra module.
An extra module that is solely trained on the current dataset is proposed.
A new set of network parameters called the expanded parameters based on weight regularization methods is introduced in AFEC.
The stability-plasticity balance in AFEC is adjusted via hyperparameters.
The authors propose to mitigate the stability-plasticity dilemma through infusing plasticity.
Plasticity is infused through the auxiliary network or module to address the stability-plasticity dilemma.
The stability-plasticity dilemma is tackled by incorporating the auxiliary network.
The auxiliary network or module is utilized to address the stability-plasticity dilemma.
The interactive mechanism between the previous model and the auxiliary model is an area that lacks a precise characterization in the literature.
The framework of CL adopts the auxiliary network called ANCL (Auxiliary Network Continual Learning) to investigate the stability-plasticity trade-off.
ANCL is empirically shown to outperform existing CL baselines on CIFAR-100 and Tiny ImageNet.
Three analyses are performed within ANCL to investigate the stability-plasticity trade-off: Weight Distance, Centered Kernel Alignment, and Mean Accuracy Landscape.
Continual learning approaches can be categorized into weight regularization, knowledge distillation, memory replay, bias correction, and dynamic structure.
Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) is a method that calculates the regularizer through the approximation of Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) in the context of Weight Regularization Method.
Memory Aware Synapses (MAS) is a method that proposes the regularizer which accumulates the changes of each parameter throughout the update history in the context of Weight Regularization Method.

Active Forgetting with synaptic Expansion-Convergence (AFEC) is a method that suggests a biologically inspired argument to propose an additional regularization term associated with expanded parameters in the context of Weight Regularization Method.
Learning without Forgetting (LwF) is a method that proposes to learn the soft target generated by the old network while learning a new task in the context of Knowledge Distillation Method.

Learning without Memorizing (LwM) is a method that is based on Learning without Forgetting (LwF) and regularizes the difference between the activations of the main network and the old network in the context of Knowledge Distillation Method.
LwM takes advantage of the attention of the previous network trained by LwF to train the current network.
DMC proposes double distillation loss to resolve the asymmetric property of training between old and new classes.
DMC proposes a new network (or auxiliary network) to resolve the asymmetric property of training between old and new classes.
DMC proposes the usage of an unlabeled auxiliary dataset to resolve the asymmetric property of training between old and new classes.
Memory replay methods are focused on keeping a part of the previous data (exemplars) in a memory buffer.
Memory replay methods keep a part of the previous data (exemplars) in a memory buffer.
iCaRL proposes the usage of the memory buffer derived from LwF.
iCaRL calculates the mean feature representations for each class based on the exemplars.
EEIL introduces an additional stage called balanced training to fine-tune the model on a balanced dataset.
The balanced dataset consists of the equal number of exemplars from each class that have been seen so far.

In memory replay methods involve training the network on a dataset composed of exemplars from previous tasks and new samples, leading to task-recency bias. Bias Correction Method aims to mitigate this bias through a two-stage training process.
Bias Correction Method and Weight Aligning both propose two-stage training processes to reduce bias in neural networks. They differ in the specific methods used for bias correction.

node_2: learning a unified classifier incrementally via rebalancing (lucir)
edge: Bias Correction Method contrasts with LUCIR by introducing a different approach to alleviating task-recency bias in machine learning models. LUCIR incorporates cosine normalization, less-forget constraint, and inter-class into its training.

node_1: learning a unified classifier incrementally via rebalancing (lucir)
edge: POD-Net builds upon the principles of LUCIR by applying pooled out distillation loss and local similarity classifier in its training process to address task-recency bias.
Dynamic Structure Method involves using masking or model expansion to prevent forgetting and enhance model capacity for learning new tasks, as seen in the case of Conditional Chan.
CCGN dynamically adds an extra convolutional layer to prevent forgetting and increase the model capacity.
AANets expands ResNet to have two types of residual block at each residual level to balance stability and plasticity.
Feature extractor is expanded to extract new features trained solely on the current dataset.
ANCL combines CL approaches with an auxiliary network to achieve a balance between stability and plasticity.
A continual learning method includes a regularizer that depends on the old network.
Before training on dataset Dt of task t, CL freezes and copies the previous continual model θCL t−1.
The old network regularizes the main training through the regularization strength λ.
We can formally define the loss of CL on task t as LCL = Lt(θ) + Ω(θ; θ∗1:t−1, λ)
The second term represents the regularizer that binds the dynamic of the network 1:t−1 to the old network parameters θ∗.
λ ∈ R is the regularization strength which is usually selected by a grid search procedure.
These two loss terms can be calculated on the current dataset Dt or on the combined dataset D+ t (current dataset Dt + previous exemplars P1:t−1) depending on the method to which it is applied.
In classification problems, the task-specific loss becomes cross-entropy loss.
The original CL approaches mainly focus on retaining the old knowledge obtained from the previous tasks.
The old CL approaches prevent large updates that would depart significantly from the old weights θ∗1:t−1.
Harmfully restricting the model's ability to learn the new knowledge might hinder the right balance between stability and plasticity.
The model's ability to learn new knowledge might be harmfully restricted.
Learning new knowledge may hinder the balance between stability and plasticity.
ANCL maintains balance by keeping two types of networks: an auxiliary network optimized for the current task and an old network trained until the previous task.
The auxiliary network is optimized for the current task, allowing for forgetting, while the old network has been sequentially trained until the previous task.
Both models (auxiliary network and old network) are used to construct regularizers in the objective of promoting the learning of new tasks.

The auxiliary network of ANCL works similarly to the expanded parameter of AFEC with respect to adding an additional loss term.

ANCL generates the loss term from the auxiliary network in the same way as the original CL where ANCL is applied, while AFEC plugs in the same loss term calculated on the expanded parameter to every method.
ANCL uses a method-dependent regularizer compared to the fixed and independent regularizer of AFEC based on Fisher Information Matrix.
ANCL adopts two regularizers of the same type to equally represent stability and plasticity, which is controlled by the scaling hyperparameters.
If the two regularizers are of different types like in AFEC, each regularizer will change in different magnitude at every epoch.
ANCL outperforms in the empirical comparison with AFEC as shown in Appendix D.

node_2: effect of λa on stability-plasticity trade-off
edge: The difference between the regularization and distillation CL methods and the effect of λa on the stability-plasticity trade-off is studied further in the following analyses
We use linear HSIC to implement CKA method for measuring the similarity of two-layer representations
It is well known that lower layers have relatively higher CKA scores than deeper layers
Lower layers have relatively higher CKA scores than deeper layers and deeper layers generally contribute to forgetting according to the context.
CKA is calculated and averaged over the set of layers in Resnet32 as mentioned in the context.
Resnet32 consists of 1 initial convolution layer and 3 residual blocks. 10 convolution layers in the last residual block of Resnet32 are selected to measure the output similarity of two networks.
Activation matrices of the ANCL network, the old network, and the auxiliary network are mentioned in relation to each other in the context.

If CKAmulti is high, the model generates layer activations similar to those of the multitask model and is highly likely to perform well on all tasks like the multitask model, as mentioned in the context.

Increasing lambda results in higher CKAaux and lower CKAold, indicating that the representations of the ANCL network become more similar to that of the auxiliary network and less similar to that of the old network, according to the context.
The stability-plasticity trade-off is controlled by λa through the interaction between the old and auxiliary networks.
If CKAmulti reaches the highest score at specific λa, that model is highly likely to have the best trade-off.

CKAmulti of the distillation methods is higher than that of the regularization methods, corresponding to the higher averaged accuracy achieved by the distillation methods compared to the regularization methods.
